title: Cornish fairings
subtitle: Crisp Cornish ginger biscuits that spread flat and crackle
excerpt: Cornish ginger biscuits made with golden syrup and ground ginger. They spread across the tray and crack on the surface as they bake. Crisp, sticky-edged, and intensely spiced. Makes about twenty biscuits.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): Cornish fairings documented as small spiced biscuits traditionally sold at Cornish fairs and markets. The name fairing applies broadly to small biscuits or confections sold at fairgrounds across the West Country. The distinctive crackled surface comes from the reaction between the bicarbonate of soda and the acidic golden syrup, a feature noted in household accounts of fair biscuit baking.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Bake in batches of ten so the biscuits have room to spread.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough balls can be chilled, covered, for up to 24 hours before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 180°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Small biscuits and sweet confections traditionally sold at country fairs and markets across Britain. In Cornwall the name became attached specifically to a crisp, spiced ginger biscuit made with golden syrup and bicarbonate of soda. The word survives primarily as a regional baking term.

content.text: are drop biscuits: no rolling, no cutter. You roll small balls of dough, drop them on the tray, and let the oven do the rest. The golden syrup and bicarbonate of soda react to produce the characteristic cracked top. The biscuits look underdone when they come out. They set as they cool.

Lard is traditional and gives a slightly crisper result than butter. Cold butter works nearly as well. Do not use soft butter: the dough will spread before the biscuits have a chance to crack properly.

items.prepNote: cold, cubed; or cold unsalted butter

content.text: Preheat the oven to 170°C fan. Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} in a large bowl. Rub in the cold {{lard}} until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs.

Warm the {{golden-syrup}} gently in a small saucepan until it is just runny, then pour it over the flour mixture. Stir until a stiff, slightly sticky dough forms. Roll into walnut-sized balls, about 15 g each. Arrange on a lined baking tray, spacing them at least 5 cm apart to allow for spreading.

Bake for 10–12 minutes, until the biscuits have spread flat and the surface is cracked and set. They will feel very soft in the centre when pressed. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack; they harden as they cool to a crisp, snappy biscuit.
